text: Five trocars are visible in the abdominal wall for the laparoscopic procedure. The surgeon performs diagnostic laparoscopy, revealing no disseminated metastatic disease. Adhesions from previous operations are lysed to facilitate additional port placement. An intraoperative ultrasound is conducted, showing no additional metastatic disease in the liver.

The surgeon scores the surface of the liver with cautery to delineate the resection boundaries. An adherent tumor focus is discovered on the distal anterior gastric wall, prompting excision with a harmonic scalpel. The left lobe of the liver is elevated, and the posterior resection boundaries are scored. Cautery energy is increased to begin deeper division of the hepatic parenchyma.

The surgeon approaches the liver from the left lateral side due to midline postoperative adhesions and port angulation. The thin lateral substance of the liver allows the use of an endo-GIA stapler with 3.5 mm staples for parenchymal transection. Gentle opposing traction and cautery are applied for deeper dissection into the hepatic parenchyma. The harmonic scalpel is used to control parenchymal division and seal larger vessels. Small peripheral vessels are dissected free, clipped proximally and distally with a laparoscopic clipapplier, and divided, continuing the parenchymal transection similar to the open finger fracture technique.

Vascular clips are applied to hepatic vessels to control minor bleeding, allowing further parenchymal transection. As the dissection progresses deeper into the hepatic parenchyma, larger segmental vessels are encountered and dissected free from surrounding tissue. An endo-GIA stapler with 2.5 mm vascular staples is used to safely divide these larger vessels. Dissection continues at the lateral edge of the falciform ligament using a harmonic scalpel until another large segmental vessel is encountered. The surrounding parenchyma is thinned to permit the application of an endoscopic stapler for vascular division, completing the separation of the specimen from the liver.

The surgeon uses high-energy cautery to coagulate the remaining raw surface of the liver, achieving hemostasis with minimal oozing. Surgicel is applied in certain areas to assist with hemostasis. The remaining gastric defect is closed transversely using a two-layer closure technique, with a running 2-0 Vicryl suture for the inner layer and interrupted 2-0 silk sutures for the outer layer.
